title: The Case Against Clean Code Aesthetics
description: How the obsession with visual polish kills personality in web design.

body.raw:
I have a confession: I'm tired of "clean" design.

Not tired of well-designed things. I appreciate craft as much as anyone. Tired of the **homogeneity** that "clean" has become. Tired of visiting twenty portfolio sites in a row that all look like they were designed by the same person with the same Figma template.

## What "Clean" Actually Means Now

In 2026, "clean design" has become code for:

- White or near-white background
- System font or Inter/Geist
- 8px grid system
- Rounded corners (8px or 12px)
- Subtle shadows
- Lots of whitespace
- Neutral color palette with one accent color

There's nothing wrong with any of these choices individually. But when everyone makes the same choices, you get **visual monoculture**. And monoculture is boring.

## The Personality Vacuum

Here's what gets lost when you optimize purely for "clean":

1. **Texture.** Real surfaces have grain, noise, imperfection. Digital surfaces don't have to be perfectly smooth.
2. **Asymmetry.** The golden ratio exists in nature. Rigid grid alignment exists in spreadsheets.
3. **Surprise.** When everything follows the same pattern, nothing stands out.
4. **Emotion.** Clean design communicates competence. It doesn't communicate joy, curiosity, or playfulness.

## The Rugrats Principle

Have you ever watched Rugrats? The art style is intentionally messy, asymmetric, and textured. It looks like a child's imagination brought to life. It's chaotic, colorful, and deeply expressive.

Now compare that to modern web design. Which one has more personality? Which one is more memorable?

I'm not saying your banking app should look like a Nickelodeon cartoon. Context matters. But if you're building a personal site, a portfolio, an art project, why would you strip away everything that makes it *yours*?

## Finding the Balance

The goal isn't chaos for chaos's sake. It's **intentional imperfection**:

- Use asymmetry, but maintain visual hierarchy
- Add texture, but preserve readability
- Break the grid, but keep the structure
- Be playful, but respect the user's time

The best designs I've seen aren't the cleanest or the messiest. They're the ones where every choice, including the "weird" ones, serves a purpose.

## Start Breaking Rules

If you're a designer or developer reading this, I challenge you: the next personal project you build, break at least three "rules." Use an unexpected font. Add texture. Make something crooked on purpose. See what happens.

Your users might not notice every choice you made. But they'll feel the difference between a site that was *designed* and a site that was *assembled from a kit*.

Make something weird. Make something yours.

title: What's the Role of Content Management Post-AI?
description: Agentic AI tools can edit content directly. Does that mean CMSs are doomed, or do they need to evolve into something stranger?

body.raw:
Last month I watched an AI agent rewrite an entire marketing site in twelve minutes. It pulled the product docs, rewrote the copy for a different audience, adjusted the tone, swapped the screenshots, and opened a pull request. No CMS was involved. No admin panel. No drag-and-drop editor. Just a language model with file access and a clear brief.

If content can be authored, edited, and published by AI agents that operate directly on codebases and file systems, what exactly is the point of a Content Management System?

## The CMS Was Born from a Constraint

Traditional CMSs exist because non-technical people needed a way to publish content on the web without touching code. WordPress, Drupal, Contentful, Sanity — they all solve the same fundamental problem: **the gap between "I have something to say" and "I need it to appear on a URL."**

That gap is closing fast. Agentic AI tools like Cursor, Windsurf, and Devin can already:

- Read a brief and produce structured content
- Edit MDX, Markdown, and HTML files directly
- Open pull requests with proposed changes
- Respond to natural language instructions like "make the tone more casual" or "add a section about pricing"

When the interface for content editing is a conversation with an AI that understands your codebase, the admin panel starts to feel like a middleman.

## Will CMSs Disappear Entirely?

No. But the ones that survive will look radically different.

Here's why the CMS won't die: **content doesn't exist in a vacuum.** A blog post isn't just words — it's words plus metadata, plus relationships to other content, plus scheduling, plus permissions, plus analytics, plus compliance, plus version history, plus multi-platform distribution. AI agents are getting good at the "words" part. They're still terrible at the orchestration layer.

The CMS of the future won't be a place where you write content. It'll be a place where content is **governed, orchestrated, and understood.**

## Five Features the Post-AI CMS Needs

If I were building a CMS today designed to survive the agentic era, here's what I'd prioritise:

### 1. Content Contracts

Every piece of content needs a machine-readable schema that defines its structure, required fields, validation rules, and relationships. Not just "title + body" — but "this product page must have a hero image between 1200×638 and 1200×800 pixels, at least three feature blocks, and a CTA that links to a valid pricing page."

AI agents need these contracts to produce valid content. Without them, an agent can write beautiful prose that completely breaks your layout because it forgot the thumbnail image.

Think of it like TypeScript for content. The CMS becomes the compiler that catches structural errors before they reach production.

### 2. Diff-Based Editorial Workflows

When an AI agent proposes a change, a human needs to review it. Not in a WYSIWYG editor — in a diff viewer. The CMS should show exactly what changed, what the implications are (SEO impact, broken links, affected translations), and let the editor approve, reject, or modify with surgical precision.

This is already how developers review code. Content review should work the same way. The CMS becomes a **merge tool for editorial decisions.**

### 3. Semantic Versioning for Content

Content needs proper version control — not the "revision history" that most CMSs offer, where you get a list of timestamps and can restore an old version. Real semantic versioning:

- **Patch**: typo fixes, metadata updates
- **Minor**: new sections, updated facts, tone adjustments
- **Major**: complete rewrites, structural changes, audience shifts

AI agents should be able to propose a version bump type, and the CMS should enforce it. This lets you reason about content stability: "this landing page hasn't had a major revision in six months" is a useful signal.

### 4. Context Feeds for Agents

AI agents need context to write good content. The CMS should be able to generate structured "context feeds" — JSON or YAML bundles that describe:

- The existing content landscape (what's already published, what's performing well)
- Brand voice guidelines (not as a PDF, but as structured rules)
- Audience data (who reads this, what they care about)
- Competitive intelligence (what similar sites are publishing)

Instead of the agent reading your entire codebase to understand context, the CMS pre-packages it. The agent gets a focused briefing document. The CMS becomes a **context broker** between humans and AI.

### 5. Content Observability

Most CMSs tell you what content exists. Almost none tell you how it's performing, whether it's consistent with the rest of the site, or when it's gone stale.

The post-AI CMS needs:

- **Drift detection**: "Your pricing page and your product page describe features differently."
- **Freshness scoring**: "This technical article references APIs that were deprecated last quarter."
- **Consistency checks**: "Three different pages use three different terms for the same concept."
- **Performance correlation**: "Pages with this content structure have 40% higher bounce rates."

AI agents can generate content at scale. The CMS needs to make sure that content doesn't silently rot.

## The Real Shift

The fundamental shift isn't "AI replaces the CMS." It's **the CMS stops being an authoring tool and becomes an orchestration platform.**

In the same way that GitHub isn't where you write code (your IDE is), the post-AI CMS isn't where you write content (your AI agent is). It's where content is:

- Validated against contracts
- Reviewed through diffs
- Versioned semantically
- Distributed across platforms
- Monitored for quality and consistency

The writing moves to the edges. The governance stays in the centre.

## What This Means for Developers

If you're building content-heavy sites today, here's my advice:

**Use file-based content.** MDX, Markdown, JSON — formats that live in your repo and are version-controlled alongside your code. This is already the native format for AI agents. Any CMS that locks content into a proprietary database is adding friction to the agentic workflow.

**Define your content schemas rigorously.** Zod schemas, TypeScript types, JSON Schema — whatever you prefer. Make the structure explicit. This is what lets AI agents produce valid content without human intervention.

**Invest in the review layer.** The bottleneck in AI-generated content isn't generation — it's review. Build or adopt tools that let humans quickly evaluate AI proposals. Diffs, previews, impact analysis.

**Don't over-index on the authoring experience.** The CMS that wins the post-AI era won't have the best WYSIWYG editor. It'll have the best governance, observability, and agent integration.

## The Sandwich Verdict

Will CMSs disappear? No. But the WordPress-style "log in and write a blog post" workflow is on borrowed time. The CMS that remains will be less like a word processor and more like an air traffic control system: coordinating, validating, and monitoring content that's primarily authored by machines, governed by contracts, and reviewed by humans.

The content isn't going away. The editing interface is just becoming a conversation.

title: Flash and the Changing Economics of Coding
description: How Gemini 3.5 Flash changes the practical economics of AI coding workflows, especially for Antigravity users on lower-tier plans.

body.raw:
Google has done something quietly important: it has released a cheaper, faster Gemini model that appears to outperform its previous Pro model on several coding and agentic benchmarks.

That matters because it challenges the simple assumption that "Pro" always means "best" and "Flash" means "acceptable, but limited." For people building apps with AI coding tools like Antigravity, the most useful model may not always be the most expensive one. It may be the model that is strong enough, fast enough, and available often enough to support real iteration.

Gemini 3.5 Flash matters because it shifts the practical question from "which model is smartest?" to "which model gives me the best working rhythm?" For hobby builders, solo creators, and people on lower-tier Google AI plans, that is often the more important question.

## Flash is no longer just the cheaper option

The traditional model hierarchy has been easy to understand.

The largest model handles difficult reasoning.
The smaller model handles speed and volume.
The cheapest model handles bulk tasks where quality matters, but not too much.

Gemini 3.5 Flash complicates that pattern.

According to Google DeepMind's published benchmark comparisons, Gemini 3.5 Flash outperforms Gemini 3.1 Pro on several coding-related and agentic tasks, including Terminal-bench 2.1, SWE-Bench Pro, Agentic MCP Atlas, OSWorld-Verified, and Finance Agent v2. It does not outperform 3.1 Pro everywhere - the Pro model remains stronger on some advanced reasoning benchmarks - but for coding workflows, the results are significant.

The useful point is not that Flash is suddenly "better than Pro" in every situation. It is that the lower-cost model now appears capable enough to carry much more of the everyday coding workload.

## Why this matters for Antigravity

Antigravity is Google's agentic development environment. It is designed around a workflow where AI agents can plan, edit, test, inspect, and iterate on code.

That kind of workflow uses a lot of model calls.

An agentic coding tool is not just answering one prompt. It may need to inspect files, propose a plan, modify code, run tests, diagnose errors, revise the implementation, and explain what changed. The value comes from the loop, not from a single impressive response.

This is where a capable Flash-tier model becomes genuinely useful.

If Gemini 3.5 Flash is strong enough for most coding iterations, users can reserve higher-tier models for the work that actually needs deeper reasoning: architecture, complex debugging, security-sensitive changes, ambiguous product decisions, or reviewing whether the whole thing is slowly becoming unmaintainable.

For users on lower-tier Google AI plans, this matters even more. If Flash access is more generous than Pro access, then Flash becomes the practical default. Not because it is the most prestigious model, but because it is the one you can use enough to keep moving.

## The real shift is iteration cost

The most important change here is not raw intelligence. It is the cost of iteration.

Most app-building work is not one perfect act of technical brilliance. It is a sequence of small and medium-sized decisions:

A component needs to be built.
An API route needs to work.
A database schema needs tightening.
The layout needs to stop looking slightly haunted.
A test fails for reasons that feel personal, even though they are probably not.

A fast, lower-cost model that performs well on coding tasks makes those loops easier to run. You can ask for smaller changes. You can test more often. You can correct course before the project turns into a cupboard full of cables that technically still belong to something.

That is the practical value.

When every model call feels expensive, users tend to overload prompts. They ask for too much at once, get a large output back, and then spend time untangling it. When the model is cheaper and still capable, the workflow can become more incremental and more controlled.

## Lower-cost coding still needs product judgment

There is a catch, of course. There is always a catch. Software development is basically a catch wearing a hoodie.

A cheaper, more capable coding model makes it easier to generate more code. That is useful, but it also makes it easier to generate too much code, too quickly, in too many directions.

This is where product judgment becomes more important, not less.

The best use of Gemini 3.5 Flash in a tool like Antigravity is not to let it build an entire product from a vague instruction and hope the result is usable. It is to give it clear constraints and use it inside a deliberate workflow:

Use it to build one feature at a time.
Ask it to make small, testable changes.
Keep the architecture simple.
Make it explain tradeoffs before it writes large amounts of code.
Use stronger models, or human review, for higher-risk decisions.

For lower-tier users, this can actually encourage a healthier approach. Instead of relying on unlimited access to the most powerful model, you build a workflow around model routing: Flash for momentum, Pro for harder reasoning, and human judgment for deciding what should exist in the first place.

Unfashionable, but useful.

## Why this is significant

Gemini 3.5 Flash is significant because it suggests that capable AI coding is becoming less tied to premium-only models.

That does not mean the largest models stop mattering. They still have an important role in complex reasoning, architecture, planning, and review. But it does mean that everyday AI-assisted development may become more accessible to people who are not on the highest-tier plans.

For Antigravity users, that could make the tool much more practical. Agentic development works best when the model can afford to loop: inspect, change, test, fix, simplify, repeat. If Flash can handle much of that loop at a lower cost, it makes the whole workflow feel less precious and more usable.

That is the quiet shift.

Not "AI can now build everything for you."
Not "developers are obsolete."
Not whatever headline has been lightly microwaved for engagement.

More simply: the cheaper model may now be good enough to do a lot of the work that makes AI coding tools useful.

## The practical takeaway

For people building apps with Antigravity, especially on lower-tier Google AI plans, Gemini 3.5 Flash may become the sensible default model to try first.

Use it for implementation, iteration, fixes, tests, and smaller refactors. Use Pro when the work needs deeper reasoning, broader context, or more careful judgment. Use your own taste and product thinking to decide whether the output is actually good.

The interesting development is not that Flash has replaced Pro. It is that the gap has narrowed enough to change how people can work.

And for solo builders, hobby developers, and people trying to turn odd little ideas into real projects without burning through their allowance of premium model calls, that is a meaningful shift.

title: On Bringing Hobbies into Code
description: How overlapping personal hobbies with software development leads to better problem statements, more interesting projects, and work that actually matters to you.

body.raw:
I have a lot of hobbies. LEGO, music production, photography, painting, writing. None of them are related to each other in any obvious way, and none of them are coding. For a long time, I treated them as separate lives: code during the day, hobbies at night, never the twain shall meet.

That was a mistake.

## The Problem with Problem Statements

Most developers I know struggle with the same thing: "what should I build?" Not technically, we can figure out the how. The hard part is the *what*. What's worth spending evenings and weekends on? What problem is actually interesting enough to sustain months of work?

The standard advice is "scratch your own itch." Find something that annoys you and fix it. And that's fine advice, but it has a ceiling. Most of the things that annoy me about daily life have already been solved by a dozen apps. I don't need another to-do list. I don't need another habit tracker.

What I needed was a better source of problem statements. And it was sitting in front of me the whole time, scattered across my apartment in the form of half-assembled LEGO sets, MIDI controllers, and oil paintings drying on the shelf.

## The Overlap Zone

Here's the thing about hobbies: they're full of friction. Not the bad kind, the *interesting* kind. The kind that makes you think "I wish there was a better way to do this" or "what if I could combine these two things" or "why does this have to be so manual?"

LEGO is about modular construction and spatial reasoning. Music production is about signal processing, pattern generation, and creative constraints. Photography is about composition, light, and perspective. Painting is about color theory, texture, and happy accidents. Writing is about structure, voice, and editing.

Every single one of those words (modular, signal, pattern, composition, texture, structure) is also a software concept. The overlap is already there. You just have to look for it.

## How Splinched Happened

Splinched started because I was frustrated with sample chopping.

I produce music as a hobby. When you're working with samples, taking a piece of audio and slicing it into smaller pieces to rearrange, the process is tedious. You load a file into a DAW, manually find the slice points, manually rearrange them, manually audition each variation. It's slow, it's repetitive, and it requires a lot of musical knowledge to do well.

I kept thinking: what if the computer could do the boring parts? What if I could drop in a sound and get back dozens of slice variations automatically? What if the rearrangement patterns were generated algorithmically instead of manually?

That question, born entirely from my hobby, from hours of manual sample chopping that I found tedious, became a full application. Browser-based audio analysis, multiple chopping algorithms, 128+ sequencer patterns, MIDI integration, WAV export. A real tool that solves a real problem.

The key insight was that I didn't start by thinking "I want to build a web app." I started by thinking "I want sample chopping to be less annoying." The technology followed the problem, not the other way around.

## Why Hobbies Make Better Problems

When you pull problem statements from your hobbies instead of from "what would look good on a resume," three things change:

**1. You actually understand the domain.**

I've spent years chopping samples by hand. I know what's annoying about it, what workflows feel natural, what features matter and what doesn't. That domain knowledge is impossible to fake and invaluable when designing a tool. Most side projects fail not because the code is bad, but because the developer doesn't deeply understand the problem they're solving.

**2. You have built-in motivation.**

Building Splinched wasn't a chore. I was solving *my own* problem. Every feature I added was something I wanted to use. That intrinsic motivation carried me through the hard parts: the audio processing edge cases, the cross-browser compatibility issues, the tedious UI work. If I'd been building yet another CRUD app for a hypothetical user, I would have quit halfway through.

**3. You can test it immediately.**

I am the user. I can load my own samples, try my own workflows, and know within seconds whether something works or feels wrong. No user interviews, no persona documents, no guessing. Just me, my music, and the tool.

## The Pattern

Once I recognized this pattern in Splinched, I started seeing it everywhere:

- **Photography** → tools for composition analysis, color palette extraction from photos, automated culling of burst shots
- **LEGO** → inventory management for brick collections, set recommendation engines, MOC (My Own Creation) instruction generators
- **Painting** → color mixing simulators, palette generators from reference images, texture overlay tools
- **Writing** → distraction-free editors with unusual constraints, prose rhythm analyzers, outline visualization tools

None of these are "solved" problems in the way that to-do lists or note-taking apps are. They're niche, personal, and rooted in real frustration from real practice. That's what makes them worth building.

## The Approach

If you want to try this yourself, here's the framework I've been using:

1. **Pick a hobby you spend real time on.** Not one you're vaguely interested in, but one where you have actual hours invested and actual frustrations accumulated.
2. **Notice the friction.** What's tedious? What's repetitive? What requires knowledge you wish you didn't need? What's manual that feels like it shouldn't be?
3. **Look for the computational angle.** Where does pattern matching, signal processing, procedural generation, data visualization, or automation apply?
4. **Build the minimum tool.** Not a platform. Not a SaaS. Just a tool that solves one specific friction point for yourself.
5. **Use it.** Actually use it. If it's good, you'll keep using it. If it's not, you'll learn why.

The goal isn't to build a startup. It's to build something you actually want to exist, and to learn a lot in the process.

## The Point

Your hobbies aren't distractions from your coding career. They're a treasure trove of domain expertise, motivation, and problem statements that most developers ignore because they don't look "professional" or "enterprise-ready."

The best projects I've built came from the intersection of "I care about this" and "I can build this." That intersection is wider than you think. You just have to stop treating your hobbies and your code as separate lives.

They're not. They're the same life. Let them overlap.

title: Turning Weird Little Ideas Into Buildable Apps
description: How to turn foggy app ideas, strange what-ifs, and creative vibes into clear requirements that AI coding tools can actually build.

body.raw:
Most app ideas do not arrive fully dressed. They turn up as sentence fragments, dumb jokes, or strange little "what ifs" your brain produces while making toast.

"What if you could hide encrypted messages inside LEGO brick images?"
"What if a song could generate printable artwork?"
"What if an app could make a full MIDI song from a genre prompt?"

This is where ideas usually die. Not because they are bad, but because they are foggy. They are vibes wearing a fake moustache and pretending to be a product.

AI coding tools make this more interesting, and also more dangerous. They can build quickly, but they are not mind readers. If you give them a vague idea, they will build a vague app. Sometimes a surprisingly confident vague app, with buttons, gradients, and the structural integrity of wet cereal.

The useful skill is not having ideas. Ideas are cheap. I have seventeen before breakfast and most of them should be contained. The useful skill is turning the idea into something buildable.

## Start with the stupid version

I try not to make the first version sound impressive. That is harder than it should be, because the instinct is to dress the idea up like it is pitching for seed funding in a room full of expensive shoes.

Do not do that.

"An app that turns messages into LEGO brick pictures, and later lets you decode them" is better than "a playful cryptographic visual communication platform leveraging modular brick-based steganographic aesthetics." The second one sounds like it was generated by a dishwasher with a LinkedIn account. The first one tells you what the thing does.

At this stage, I want one or two plain sentences. Enough to give the idea a spine.

## Find the actual interaction

A lot of app ideas are secretly just outputs. "Generate artwork." "Make a MIDI file." "Encode a message." That is not an app yet. That is a vending machine.

The app is the interaction around the output. What does the user do first? What do they see while it is working? What do they get at the end? What happens if it fails? What makes them want to try again?

Take the LEGO cryptography idea. The output is a PNG image. Fine. But the interaction is the product: write a secret message, enter a password, encrypt it, generate a pleasing abstract LEGO object, download the image, then later upload it and decode the message.

Now it has a shape. Not a perfect one, but it has hands and feet. It can walk around a bit.

## Set the rules of the game

This might be the most important part. AI tools are very good at adding things. They will happily give your tiny weird app user accounts, dashboards, analytics, onboarding tours, Stripe integration, and a testimonial carousel from imaginary people named "Sarah K."

You need constraints. No login unless absolutely necessary. No database unless the app truly needs persistence. No AI image generation if the concept should be algorithmic. Must run on Vercel serverless. Must work on mobile. Must produce downloadable files. Must be understandable from the first screen.

This is a bit like building a Magic: The Gathering deck. The interesting part is not having access to every card ever printed. That way lies nonsense, bankruptcy, and a shoebox full of goblins. The interesting part is choosing a format, a colour identity, a win condition, and the cards you are deliberately not playing.

Apps are the same. Constraints are not boring. They are what stop the thing becoming a cursed train.

## Turn vibes into requirements

Vibes are allowed. I like vibes. Aesthetic direction matters, especially for small creative tools. But vibes need translation.

"Make it whimsical" is not enough. Whimsical how? Soft colours? Bouncy motion? Hand-drawn icons? Casual copy? Rounded cards? Does it look like a children's book, a toy catalogue, a 90s CD-ROM, or a slightly unwell spreadsheet?

Instead of "late 90s hacking vibe," write: "Dark panels, chunky pixel-inspired borders, faux terminal elements, sparse neon accents, monospace labels, playful warning copy, and simple animated scan states. Avoid modern SaaS minimalism."

That gives the AI something to build. It also tells it what to avoid, which is often more useful. Left unattended, AI will make everything look like a password manager for a Scandinavian bank.

## Write the PRD like instructions for a very fast intern

An AI-readable PRD does not need to be fancy. Fancy is usually bad. The goal is not to impress a steering committee. The goal is to reduce ambiguity.

I write PRDs as if I am handing the project to a capable intern who works very fast, has read the entire internet, and occasionally forgets what gravity is.

The structure is simple: product name, one-line summary, goal, target user, core journey, functional requirements, technical constraints, UI direction, edge cases, and acceptance criteria.

The acceptance criteria matter. "The app should generate nice artwork" is useless. "The app should generate a deterministic PNG from the uploaded audio, and the same audio file should always produce the same image" is testable. Now the AI has a target, and so do you.

## Name the hard parts

A lot of AI-built app projects go wrong because the PRD says something breezy like, "The app generates a finished, professional-quality song."

Right. Of course. Just pop that in a button.

Some things are hard. Strong encryption is hard. Good procedural music is hard. Beautiful deterministic art is hard. Running everything inside serverless limits can be hard. Accessibility is not a garnish.

A good PRD names the traps. "Use standard browser cryptography APIs. Do not invent a custom encryption algorithm." Or: "Image generation must be algorithmic and deterministic. Do not use AI image generation."

This is not pessimism. It is putting little flags next to the holes.

## Build the smallest impressive version

I do not mean an MVP where you remove everything joyful and ship a beige apology. I mean the smallest version that proves the idea.

For the LEGO cryptography app, that might be: write message, encrypt it, generate PNG, upload PNG, decrypt message. No accounts. No gallery. No teams. No social graph. Please, for the love of small apps, no social graph.

The mistake is trying to build the fantasy version first. That way lies a half-finished settings panel and a deep sense of personal failure.

Small and complete beats ambitious and haunted.

## The PRD is the spellbook

The weird idea is the spark. The PRD is how you stop the spark from setting fire to the curtains.

The process is simple: capture the plain idea, define the core interaction, set constraints, translate vibes into buildable requirements, name the hard parts, define acceptance criteria, build the smallest impressive version, then test it like a suspicious raccoon.

That is how a vague concept becomes a buildable app. Not by making it bigger. Usually by making it smaller, clearer, and less able to escape through the vents.

The best weird little apps do not need to become startups. They do not need pricing pages, growth loops, or inspirational launch posts with laptops near plants. Some apps can just be useful, playful, or strange little artefacts that exist because the idea made you grin for three seconds and you had enough discipline to turn it into a thing.

That is enough. Honestly, it might be better.

title: Why I Build Weird Things
description: On the importance of play, experimentation, and not taking your portfolio too seriously.

body.raw:
Every time I sit down to build something for my portfolio, I face the same temptation: make it clean, make it professional, make it look like every other developer portfolio out there. Dark mode. Sans-serif font. Grid of project cards. Maybe a subtle gradient.

And every time, I resist.

Not because there's anything wrong with clean design. Clean design is hard to do well. But because **my portfolio should feel like me**, and I am not a clean, minimal, corporate-speak kind of person.

## The Problem with Professionalism

There's a pervasive idea in tech that "professional" means "sterile." That your portfolio should look like it was designed by the same agency that did a SaaS dashboard. That personality is risky.

I think the opposite is true. **Personality is memorable.** The portfolio that makes someone say "what the hell is this?" and then spend 10 minutes exploring is infinitely more valuable than the one that gets a polite nod and a closed tab.

## What Play Produces

When you build things for fun, without worrying about whether they'll impress a hiring manager, you learn faster. You try weirder techniques. You combine technologies in unexpected ways. You make mistakes that lead to discoveries.

My Pixel Garden project started because I wondered "what if cellular automata could evolve?" My Voice Canvas came from a late-night experiment with the Web Audio API. Neither was planned as a portfolio piece. Both taught me more than any tutorial.

## The Sandwich Philosophy

I call my approach "sandwich codes" because:

1. **Layers matter.** A good sandwich has texture, contrast, surprise. So does good software.
2. **It's handmade.** You can taste the difference between a hand-assembled sandwich and one made by a machine. Users can feel the difference between handcrafted software and template-driven development.
3. **It's not precious.** Nobody agonizes over the perfect sandwich. You make it, you eat it, you make a different one next time. Software should be the same: build it, ship it, learn from it, build something else.

## Build Weird Things

If you're reading this and you have a half-finished weird project sitting in a repo somewhere, finish it. Ship it. Don't polish it until it's indistinguishable from everything else. Let it be strange. Let it be yours.

The world has enough clean portfolios. It needs more weird ones.
